metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.finding: Sibling tickets touch the same file(s) with no ordering edge.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a depends_on/blocks edge to serialize, or partition the file ownership.
Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the concrete artifact that constitutes 'captured as an idea' (e.g., 'a GitHub issue with label `idea` is filed for each deferred item') so the AC is unambiguously verifiable in-session.
findings.finding: The AC 'Lower-value inventory work (Python-API / config-key) is captured as an idea, not shipped speculatively' names no concrete artifact type, making in-session verification ambiguous — there is no stated observable artifact (e.g. a filed GitHub issue, a doc entry) an executor or reviewer can point to as proof of completion.
findings.suggested_fix: Append to the bullet: 'Concretely: a GitHub issue tagged `idea` is opened for each (Python-API inventory, config-key inventory) and linked here; no code implementing either lands in this epic.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define 'live registries' for CLI and MCP at the epic level (e.g., 'the registry is the list of commands/tools produced by running `<generator script>` against the live source') so the drift-gate AC is self-contained and measurable without opening child tickets.
findings.finding: The AC 'Generated CLI + MCP references drift-gate against their live registries' uses the undefined term 'live registries' — it is not stated what artifact or source-of-truth constitutes the 'live registry' for CLI and MCP references at the epic level, making the criterion hard to evaluate without reading child tickets e866/235a.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — 'Generated CLI + MCP references drift-gate' bullet
findings.suggested_fix: Expand the bullet to: 'Generated CLI reference (ticket e866) and MCP tool-list reference (ticket 235a) each have a CI drift gate: the generator script is run in CI and its output is diffed against the committed reference; a non-empty diff fails the build. The source of truth is the live Python entry-points / MCP tool registry at HEAD.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the contradiction between the Reuse note (probe-rebar.sh 'just is not wired into CI') and AC5 ('not merely an independent probe with its own syntax') by clarifying probe-rebar.sh's role: either (a) state it is superseded by the README-extraction gate and will not be wired in directly, or (b) describe how it is adapted/invoked by the README-extraction gate.
findings.finding: The Context 'Reuse note' frames probe-rebar.sh as the ready-made implementation ('already implements the full golden path; it just is not wired into CI'), implying the approach is to wire it in. But AC5 and the Drift-proofing mechanism sub-section explicitly state the gate must run 'the literal commands extracted from the README quickstart fenced block (not merely an independent probe with its own syntax)' — the parenthetical directly contrasts probe-rebar.sh's approach. An implementer reading Context first would start by wiring in the existing script; reaching AC5 they discover they must build a README-extraction mechanism instead. These two sections send the implementer in different directions.
findings.location: Context > Reuse note (probe-rebar.sh paragraph) vs. Acceptance Criteria AC5 + Drift-proofing mechanism sub-section
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence to the Reuse note clarifying the relationship: e.g., 'probe-rebar.sh serves as prior art and infrastructure reference; the CI gate (ticket 0435) does NOT wire it in directly — instead it extracts and runs the fenced README commands, so a wrong printed command fails the build.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add at least one explicit AC (or expand an existing AC) to cover the two orphaned P1 remediations: (a) stale bash-engine language removed from CI comments and leaf.py, and (b) docs/output-schemas.md updated with the correct source path and without the removed bash shell-out description.
findings.finding: Two P1 validated findings explicitly listed in Context — (1) 'stale bash-engine language in CI comments + leaf.py' and (2) 'docs/output-schemas.md points at a moved source path and describes a removed bash shell-out' — have no dedicated epic-level Acceptance Criteria. Their only coverage is the catch-all AC1 ('All child tickets are closed'), which provides no verification mechanism at epic-close that these specific remediations were completed and not inadvertently scoped out of child tickets.
findings.location: Context > Validated findings (P1 bullets) vs. Acceptance Criteria section
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC such as: '- [ ] `docs/output-schemas.md` references the current source path and contains no description of the removed bash shell-out; stale bash-engine language is removed from CI workflow comments and `leaf.py`.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback/back-out plan for the `docs/archive/` relocation in ticket `9d81` (e.g., a pre-move audit of all inbound references repo-wide, a git-revert instruction, or HTTP redirect stubs), so that the destructive path move is an explicit, reversible choice rather than an acknowledged-but-unmitigated one.
findings.finding: The `docs/archive/` relocation performed by ticket `9d81` is a destructive, partially irreversible step (history is preserved in git, but canonical URLs/paths change permanently), and the plan provides no explicit rollback or back-out procedure. The CI gate (`f088`) is scoped only to 'links this epic manages' — any inbound links outside that scope (external sites, other repo docs, README badges, tooling configs) that point to the pre-move paths are silently stranded with no recovery path described.
findings.location: AC — docs-index completeness gate paragraph: 'relative links the remediation touches (index entries + inbound links to docs relocated under `docs/archive/` by `9d81`) resolve. Scope is bounded to index completeness and the links this epic manages'
findings.suggested_fix: Before merging `9d81`, run `grep -r 'docs/<relocated-filename>' . --include='*.md' --include='*.yml' --include='*.py'` to enumerate all inbound references beyond the epic's managed scope; add any found paths to `f088`'s link-check, or add a note to `docs/README.md` redirecting readers. Document the git-revert command as the back-out if post-merge issues are found.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify, within ticket `0435` or the shared CI workflow, where the extraction contract is documented for contributors (e.g., a comment block in the CI step, a CONTRIBUTING.md note, or an inline README annotation), so that the fence-extraction convention is discoverable without reading implementation source.
findings.finding: The golden-path CI gate (ticket `0435`) introduces a new, non-obvious CI contract: it extracts fenced command blocks from the README quickstart and executes them verbatim. Future contributors who edit the README quickstart or add new fenced blocks have no documented guidance on what the gate expects (which fences are extracted, ordering, required markers, environment assumptions). Without this, contributors will inadvertently break CI and have no doc to consult.
findings.location: AC — 'Drift-proofing mechanism' section and the golden-path AC line: 'extract the fenced command block(s) from the README quickstart and execute those exact lines'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a comment block at the top of the golden-path CI step (in `.github/workflows/test.yml`) that states: which section heading delimits the quickstart, which fence type/marker is extracted vs. skipped (e.g., only ` ```sh ` or ` ```bash ` blocks), and what environment the commands run in. Reference this comment from CONTRIBUTING.md under 'Editing the quickstart'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a CLAUDE.md / contributing-guide note (or ADR) documenting the README quickstart authorship contract: that the fenced command block(s) are extracted and executed verbatim by CI, what constraints that imposes (no interactive commands, no unexported env vars, block identification heuristic used), and how to update the gate when the quickstart changes.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a novel CI contract — README quickstart fenced blocks must be extractable and directly executable in CI — but does not specify capturing this architectural decision in CLAUDE.md, a contributing guide, or an ADR. Future contributors who edit the README quickstart won't know their commands are machine-executed by CI, risking inadvertent breakage (e.g. adding context-setting prose inside a fenced block, using interactive commands, or relying on local env vars not present in CI).
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — 'The golden-path CI gate runs the literal commands extracted from the README quickstart fenced block' + 'Drift-proofing mechanism' section
findings.suggested_fix: In the ticket that implements the golden-path CI gate (0435), add an AC line: 'Update CLAUDE.md (or docs/contributing.md) with a "README quickstart CI contract" section explaining that the fenced block is executed verbatim in CI, listing authoring constraints, and describing how to update the extraction logic if the quickstart structure changes.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC for the audience-router / living-vs-historical separation that makes it end-state-observable (e.g., 'The docs index contains a labeled audience-router section with entries for all four named audiences, and living docs are visually/structurally separated from archived/historical material').
findings.finding: The title's third promise — 'clarify taxonomy' — maps to the intent-based audience router and living-vs-historical index separation, described in Success Criteria but absent from the Acceptance Criteria, leaving it without an end-state-observable pass/fail proof.
findings.location: Success Criteria (audience router bullet) vs. Acceptance Criteria (no corresponding AC)
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC such as: '[ ] docs/README.md contains an explicit audience-router section (or equivalent labeled navigation aid) addressing all four named audiences (human clients, LLM clients, human contributors, LLM contributors), and living normative docs are structurally distinguished from archived historical evidence (e.g., under docs/archive/). Verified by human review of the rendered index at epic close.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Ticket `0435` (or the epic's drift-proofing mechanism section) should explicitly specify that the extracted quickstart block executes as a **single shell script** (e.g., written to a temp file and invoked with `bash -e <file>`, or passed as a heredoc to `bash`) so that shell variable assignments persist across lines; document this in the AC so the implementer doesn't pick line-by-line execution.
findings.finding: The 'execute those exact lines' mechanism in ticket 0435 has an unspecified execution model that hides an ordering/atomicity assumption: if the repaired quickstart block (from b55f) assigns the created ticket id to a shell variable across multiple lines (e.g. `TID=$(rebar create … --output json | jq -r .id)` on line 1, `rebar claim $TID …` on line 2), the extractor must run the block as a SINGLE SCRIPT (temp file or heredoc passed to bash) rather than line-by-line. Line-by-line execution would lose the variable, causing the claim/transition steps to silently use an empty id — a spurious CI failure or a wrong-command error that does not reflect a README defect. Neither the epic plan nor the ticket AC specifies which execution model the extractor uses.
findings.location: Drift-proofing mechanism section; ticket 0435 (contained-dendritic-angora) AC and description; ticket b55f AC
findings.suggested_fix: In ticket 0435 AC, add: 'The extractor runs the extracted block as a single bash script (e.g. writes lines to a temp file and invokes `bash -e <tmpfile>`) — NOT line-by-line — so shell variable assignments (e.g. `TID=$(…)`) persist across commands.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in ticket e866 that `gen_cli_reference.py` should enumerate subcommands and usage strings from the package-data help corpus (`src/rebar/_cli/help/*.txt` via `known_subcommands()` in `_cli/_help.py`), not from a 'CLI registry' object (which does not exist as a single importable entity).
findings.finding: The gen_cli_reference.py generator (ticket e866) asserts it will 'derive the command list, usage strings, and flags from the CLI registry / --help', but no such 'CLI registry' Python object exists. The CLI help system stores usage strings as pinned package-data .txt files under src/rebar/_cli/help/*.txt, enumerable via `known_subcommands()` in `_cli/_help.py`. An implementor targeting a non-existent registry object will either reinvent enumeration or shell out to `rebar --help` (which only surfaces the overview). The actual source-of-truth mechanism for the generator is unverified.
findings.location: ticket e866 (decorative-kingsize-degu) — What / AC sections; src/rebar/_cli/_help.py known_subcommands(); src/rebar/_cli/help/*.txt
findings.suggested_fix: Update e866's What section: 'derive the command list and usage strings by enumerating the package-data help files under `src/rebar/_cli/help/` via `rebar._cli._help.known_subcommands()` (the same enumeration the CLI itself uses); per-command flag detail comes from parsing the corresponding .txt files.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve in ticket 235a how `gen_mcp_reference.py` will enumerate ALL tool profiles: specify whether it calls `build_server()` multiple times under different env configurations (REBAR_MCP_READONLY=0/1, REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_LLM=0/1) or statically inspects the registrar source files (`_mcp_reads.py`, `_mcp_writes.py`, `_mcp_llm.py`) — and verify that the chosen mechanism can distinguish conditionally-registered tools from always-registered-but-runtime-gated tools.
findings.finding: The gen_mcp_reference.py generator (ticket 235a) asserts it will 'enumerate tools from the MCP server's `list_tools()` registry' and 'correctly separate read-only from write/live-gated tools', but this is an unverified capability assertion: `build_server()` / `list_tools()` output depends on env vars (REBAR_MCP_READONLY, REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_LLM, REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_JIRA_SYNC) that gate which tools are registered. A single invocation of `list_tools()` with neutral env yields only the baseline tool set; getting the full profile picture requires multiple server builds under different env configurations. The plan provides no mechanism for this.
findings.location: ticket 235a (lupine-clearcut-mare) — What / AC sections; src/rebar/mcp_server.py; src/rebar/_mcp_writes.py; src/rebar/_mcp_llm.py

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the concrete strategy in ticket `235a` for enumerating gated MCP tools across both profiles (e.g. call `build_server()` twice — once with `REBAR_MCP_READONLY=1` and once without — and diff the tool lists, OR enumerate the three registrar modules statically and annotate each tool's gate condition), so the implementation is unambiguous and the AC's per-profile correctness is verifiable.
findings.finding: The MCP reference generator (ticket `235a`) states it will enumerate tools from the MCP server's `list_tools()` registry, but FastMCP conditionally registers write tools at `build_server()` time based on `REBAR_MCP_READONLY` — a naive single call to `build_server().list_tools()` in CI (default non-readonly) would enumerate all tools and not produce the correct per-profile split the AC requires. The ticket names the required outcome (read-only vs write/live-gated distinction) but leaves the generation strategy for conditionally-registered tools unspecified, creating an implementation risk without a spike.
findings.location: Child ticket `235a` (lupine-clearcut-mare) — AC: 'correctly separates read-only from write/live-gated tools'
findings.suggested_fix: In the ticket `235a` description, add an implementation note: 'The generator must enumerate tools under BOTH profiles by calling build_server() with REBAR_MCP_READONLY=1 (read-only) and REBAR_MCP_READONLY unset (full); the set difference identifies write/gated tools. Alternatively, enumerate _mcp_reads, _mcp_llm, and _mcp_writes modules statically, reading each @mcp.tool decorator and its surrounding guard comment to derive the profile annotation.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define ticket `e205` (referenced in the `.github/workflows/test.yml` edit-ordering chain but not found in the repo) or correct the plan to name the actual ticket that occupies that slot in the serialization stack.
findings.finding: Ticket `e205` is named in the plan's 'Shared-file edit ordering' section as one of five tickets editing `.github/workflows/test.yml` (alongside `0435`, `e866`, `235a`, `f088`), but no ticket with that prefix exists anywhere in the repo — show_ticket returns nothing, and no file under the codebase or ticket store contains `e205-` or the alias. The serialization ordering the plan documents is therefore incomplete: an executor cannot verify or follow the stack order for the `.github/workflows/test.yml` shared file because one link in the chain is undefined.
findings.suggested_fix: Verify whether `e205` was intended to be the ticket for 'P1: Fix stale bash-engine language in CI comments + leaf.py' (the validated P1 finding with no current child ticket). If so, create that ticket and add it to the dependency chain between `b55f`/`319a` and `e866`. If the ordering was an authoring error and no such workflow edit is needed, remove `e205` from the serialization description.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] 235a must either update CLAUDE.md to point to docs/mcp-reference.md (removing or replacing its hand-maintained tool catalog) or explicitly hand off that de-duplication to a named sibling (e.g., b5a4), and the files list must reflect whichever child owns it.
findings.finding: 235a states its motivation as preventing CLAUDE.md's hand-maintained MCP catalog from drifting, but its Acceptance Criteria and files list contain no action on CLAUDE.md. No sibling ticket addresses CLAUDE.md's MCP tool catalog. The generated docs/mcp-reference.md is created, but CLAUDE.md retains a parallel hand-maintained catalog that can independently go stale — exactly the drift failure mode the ticket claims to eliminate.
findings.suggested_fix: Add CLAUDE.md to 235a's files list with an AC requiring the hand-maintained MCP tool catalog section to be replaced with a pointer to docs/mcp-reference.md, OR add an AC to b5a4 ('trim duplicated catalogs') that explicitly includes CLAUDE.md's MCP catalog alongside README.md's catalogs.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `depends_on: 9d81` to child 6755's dependency declarations so the edit ordering for docs/README.md is enforced at the ticket level, not only by the parent's prose.
findings.finding: Child 6755 edits docs/README.md and adds router/troubleshooting links, but declares no depends_on 9d81, even though 9d81 also edits docs/README.md first (parent serialization: 'edited by 9d81, 6755'). If 6755 executes before 9d81 in any non-single-agent execution path, 6755's changes would be based on the pre-9d81 index state, causing a merge conflict or requiring a rebase against 9d81's structural reorganization (new sections, archive relocations, taxonomy headings).
findings.suggested_fix: In child 6755's ticket metadata/description, add an explicit 'Depends on 9d81 (docs/README.md structural reorganization must land first)' line, mirroring how 0435 declares depends_on a41f and b55f.

findings.finding: The plan silently drops the `relates_to` link to ticket ec52-b249-5b98-4027 that is explicitly stated in the session log's ticket graph, with no mention of what the relationship entails, whether it constrains scope, or a rationale for ignoring it.

Child `contained-dendritic-angora` is titled and described as 'wire the existing golden-path probe into CI' (i.e., running probe-rebar.sh), but the epic's AC and the Drift-proofing mechanism section explicitly require a DIFFERENT artifact: a gate that extracts fenced command blocks from the README quickstart and runs those exact lines. The plan itself explains why these are not equivalent — probe-rebar.sh uses its own internally-correct syntax and does NOT catch wrong text printed in the README. As scoped, the child would deliver a CI gate that passes even when the README contains the broken `session-log` command, contradicting the epic's stated G6/F4 mechanism.
findings.location: Child ticket `contained-dendritic-angora` title/description vs. AC block 'The golden-path gate … is scoped to extract the fenced command block(s) from the README quickstart and execute those exact lines' and 'Reuse note: scripts/probe-rebar.sh already implements the full install→…→close golden path; it just is not wired into CI'
findings.suggested_fix: Update the child title to 'P0: Add CI gate that extracts and executes README quickstart fenced block verbatim' and in its description distinguish the two sub-tasks: (1) author/adapt the extraction script, and (2) wire it into CI — making clear that probe-rebar.sh may be a reference implementation but is not the gate itself.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
